reason: Response B's clipped, punchy fragments ("The machine has taste." "The sock is gone. It crossed over.") and escalating deadpan rhythm fit Haiku's compact, high-density comedic style better than A's looser, more elaborate single-narrative paragraphs. A reads like a larger model padding out one bit with extended scene-building and qualifiers ("literally," "the whole nine yards"), whereas B builds distinct comedic beats economically, which is characteristic of Haiku working within tighter generative tendencies. The "grief management"/"humility" turn in B also shows the kind of concise tonal pivot Haiku tends to produce.
